ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for vegetable fried noodles; 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002700) is the closest defensible broader class. A more specific term for stir-fried noodle dishes is not available in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible match. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for stir-fried vegetable noodles as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for vegetable fried noodles as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT clinical food coding does not extend to this level of culinary specificity.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (cabbage, carrots, capsicum, bean sprouts)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of vegetable fried noodles is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served in Singapore hawker centres and Chinese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Noodles with Vegetables (Chow Mein) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for stir-fried vegetable noodles / fried noodles with vegetables. HPB Foodo reports nutrient values per 100 g for cooked stir-fried noodle dishes with vegetables. The dish described (Hakka noodles, mixed vegetables, soy sauce, spring onions, garlic, ginger) is consistent with the HPB reference entry for fried noodles with vegetables. Minor variation is expected across restaurant preparations. Where HPB Foodo does not report a micronutrient, the value is set to -1 per Veritas sentinel policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Noodles, wheat-based, cooked; Chow Mein noodles analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetable fried Hakka noodles as a composite dish. The GI of 47 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked wheat-based noodles (GI ~47, low category), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The vegetable and soy sauce components do not materially raise the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (47 × 61.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 ≈ 28.9, rounded to 29.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1,260 mg sodium (about 55% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg), primarily from soy sauce; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium soy sauce and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a low GI of approximately 47, the large carbohydrate load in a standard 300 g serving (approximately 61.5 g carbohydrate, GL ≈ 29) is moderate-to-high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based dish with vegetable oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vegetable fried noodles is a plant-based dish with low saturated fat (~0.7 g per 100 g) and no cholesterol, making it a relatively heart-friendly option; however, sodium content from soy sauce should be monitored, and oil quantity controlled during preparation to keep total fat intake within cardiovascular guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Fried Noodles (蔬菜炒面, Shūcài Chǎomiàn) is a popular Chinese stir-fried noodle dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres, Chinese restaurants, and Indian-Chinese establishments. The dish is made with Hakka-style wheat flour noodles that are wok-tossed over high heat with mixed vegetables such as cabbage, carrots, capsicum, and bean sprouts, seasoned with soy sauce, and flavoured with spring onions, garlic, and ginger. The cooking technique — rapid stir-frying in a hot wok with a small amount of vegetable oil — produces noodles with a slightly chewy texture and a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with mild smokiness from wok hei. The dish is fully vegetarian and vegan, contains no meat or dairy, and is suitable for halal diets. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 390 kcal, 10.5 g protein, 11.4 g fat, and 61.5 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 47 (low), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 29 due to the large noodle portion. Sodium content is a clinical concern, with approximately 1,260 mg per serving from soy sauce, relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish provides modest dietary fibre (~4.5 g per serving) from the mixed vegetables. It is a staple comfort food in Singapore's multicultural food landscape, appearing on menus across Chinese, Indian-Chinese, and fusion restaurants.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for stir-fried vegetable noodle dishes, which is the closest approved primary source for this Singapore-served Chinese dish. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked wheat-based noodles, as no direct GI measurement for vegetable fried Hakka noodles exists in the approved GI database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo for fried noodles with vegetables (stir-fried, cooked per 100 g). Exact values may vary across restaurant preparations depending on oil quantity, soy sauce volume, and vegetable mix. 2. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate based on cooked wheat-based noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct GI measurement for vegetable fried Hakka noodles is available in the approved source. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish category. 4. folate_mcg_dfe is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish category. 5. vitamin_e_mg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish category. 6. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish category. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no vitamin D-containing ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. 9. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. 10. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as vegetable oil used in stir-frying does not contribute trans fats; confirmed zero by formulation. 11. The input cuisine_tags included irrelevant tags (e.g., Butter Chicken, Biryani, Curry) from restaurant metadata; these have been disregarded as they reflect multi-cuisine field dataings rather than dish classification. 12. Food group has been reclassified from 'Vegetables' (input) to 'Grains and staples' as the primary caloric component is wheat noodles.
